county_metadata.record_losses: Austin (former county seat) courthouse fire risk was a concern but no major losses documented. Records are largely intact.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created from the original St. Mary's County in 1862 (St. Mary's was one of the original 9 counties from 1861 but was renamed). Austin silver rush (1862) drove formation. County seat was Austin until 1979 when it moved to Battle Mountain. Eureka County (1873), Elko portion, and White Pine portion were carved from Lander.
county_metadata.research_tips: Lander County was once much larger, so early territorial records filed here may actually pertain to areas now in Eureka, White Pine, or Elko counties. Austin was the county seat from 1862 to 1979, and the Lander County Courthouse in Austin still exists as a museum — some historical records remain there while active records moved to Battle Mountain. The Austin silver rush (Reese River Mining District, 1862) is well documented in the Reese River Reveille newspaper, one of Nevada's most important territorial newspapers. Mining claim records at the county recorder cover the extensive Reese River district. Austin had Chinese, Italian, and Cornish mining communities — check Chinese Exclusion Act files at NARA San Bruno and fraternal organization records. The Central Nevada Museum in Tonopah and UNR Special Collections have Lander County mining materials. FamilySearch has microfilmed marriage and other county records from the 1860s.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: No online access; county recorder in Battle Mountain handles in-person requests

source_profiles.source_name: Lander County Recorder — Land and Mining Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deeds and mining claims from territorial era. Reese River Mining District claims are extensive. Pre-1873 records may cover Eureka County area.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete — records transferred from Austin to Battle Mountain in 1979.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: One of Nevada's premier territorial newspapers. Mining notices, legal advertisements, social news, and territorial politics from Austin.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good digitization coverage — a premier source for central Nevada territorial research.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from territorial era. Includes records from Austin boomtown period.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — early territorial records survive.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census 1870–1950 (1890 destroyed). Note large 1870 population (Austin boom), then decline. Pre-1873 may include Eureka area.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete for surviving years.

source_profiles.repository: Lander County District Court, Battle Mountain
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, estates, guardianships from territorial era. Early records filed at Austin courthouse.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well preserved; transferred to Battle Mountain with other county records.

county_metadata.record_losses: Pioche courthouse was poorly constructed (the 'Million Dollar Courthouse' scandal) but records survived. No major fire losses.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed from Nye County in 1866. Named for Abraham Lincoln. Originally much larger — Clark County carved out in 1909. Pioche silver mining boom in the 1870s was notoriously violent. LDS settlements in Panaca (1864) and surrounding areas predate the mining boom. Lincoln County once extended to the Colorado River.
county_metadata.research_tips: Lincoln County research splits between two very different communities: the LDS agricultural settlements (Panaca, Eagle Valley, Caliente) and the rough mining camp of Pioche. For LDS families, the Church History Library in Salt Lake City has ward records for the Panaca and Meadow Valley settlements from the 1860s — these are exceptionally detailed for birth, marriage, and death records. Pioche's mining boom (1870s) was legendarily violent and generated extensive court records including inquests and criminal cases. The Lincoln County Recorder in Pioche has deeds from 1866. Pre-1909 records here cover the entire area that became Clark County, so Las Vegas-area researchers must check Lincoln County for early records. The Lincoln County Museum in Pioche preserves mining and pioneer history. The infamous 'Million Dollar Courthouse' (built 1871, not paid off until 1937) is itself a landmark. FamilySearch has microfilmed LDS ward records and county vital records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: No online access; county recorder in Pioche handles in-person requests

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deeds from county formation. Pre-1909 records cover area that became Clark County. Mining claims from Pioche district.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete — no major losses despite courthouse construction problems.

source_profiles.source_name: LDS Ward Records — Panaca, Meadow Valley, Eagle Valley
source_profiles.repository: Church History Library, Salt Lake City / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Ward membership, blessings, baptisms, marriages for LDS communities in Lincoln County. Panaca Ward records are among the earliest in Nevada.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent — LDS Church archives preserve comprehensive ward records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from county formation. Pre-1909 includes Clark County area marriages.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — continuous from 1866.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census 1870–1950 (1890 destroyed). Pre-1909 census covers Clark County area. Note both mining and LDS agricultural populations.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete for surviving years.

source_profiles.source_name: Lincoln County District Court — Criminal and Civil Records
source_profiles.repository: Lincoln County Courthouse, Pioche / Nevada State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: District court records including Pioche mining camp violence cases, inquests, and mining dispute litigation.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — older records at state archives.

source_profiles.source_name: Pioche Record and Lincoln County Record
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Pioche mining-era newspapers with legal notices, mining claims, obituaries, and court proceedings.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial digitization; microfilm at Nevada State Library.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major record losses. County seat moved multiple times, but records were preserved through each move.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of Nevada's original nine counties created in 1861. Named for Nathaniel Lyon, first Union general killed in the Civil War. County seat moved from Dayton to Yerington in 1911. Dayton is one of Nevada's oldest settlements (Gold Canyon, 1849). Mineral County carved out in 1911. Fort Churchill (now a state park) was in Lyon County.
county_metadata.research_tips: Lyon County encompasses some of Nevada's earliest gold mining areas — Gold Canyon near Dayton is where the Comstock Lode was first discovered before the main lode was found in Virginia City. The Dayton area has records from the late 1840s–1850s predating Nevada Territory. The Lyon County Recorder has deeds from the territorial era. County seat moved from Dayton to Yerington in 1911, so pre-1911 court and probate records were filed in Dayton. Fort Churchill military records (1860–1869) document soldiers in the Lyon County area. The Mason Valley (Yerington) became an agricultural and ranching center, and the Anaconda Copper smelter at Yerington (1911–1978) created industrial employment records. The Walker River Paiute Tribe reservation is partially in Lyon County — BIA records at NARA cover this community. FamilySearch has microfilmed county vital records. Check Mineral County for post-1911 records from the Hawthorne/Walker Lake area that was formerly Lyon County.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County recorder land records; limited online access, most records require in-person visit

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deeds from territorial era including early Gold Canyon mining claims. Pre-1911 records include Mineral County area.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete — preserved through county seat moves.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from territorial era. Some of Nevada's earliest civil marriage records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — continuous from early territorial period.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Post returns, muster rolls, and garrison records from Fort Churchill. Documents Civil War-era soldiers on the Nevada frontier.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — NARA holds complete post records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census 1870–1950 (1890 destroyed). Pre-1911 includes Mineral County area. Note early Gold Canyon settlers in 1870 census.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete for surviving years.

source_profiles.source_name: Walker River Paiute Tribe — BIA Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Bureau of Indian Affairs records for the Walker River Paiute Reservation. Census rolls, allotment records, and agency correspondence.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — NARA holds BIA records for western Nevada agencies.

source_profiles.source_name: Lyon County Times and Mason Valley News
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Local newspapers from Dayton, Yerington, and Mason Valley. Mining, ranching, and agricultural news.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial digitization; microfilm at Nevada State Library.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major record losses. Pre-1911 records split between Esmeralda County (Goldfield) and Lyon County (Yerington).
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed from Esmeralda County in 1911, with portions also from Lyon County. Named for the mining industry. Hawthorne Army Ammunition Depot (established 1930) became the largest ammunition storage facility in the world. Walker Lake and Wassuk Range define the landscape. Small county population.
county_metadata.research_tips: Mineral County requires tracing records through multiple parent counties: Esmeralda County (Goldfield) for the eastern portion and Lyon County (Yerington) for the western portion prior to 1911. The Hawthorne Army Ammunition Depot (1930–present) was a major employer — military civilian employment records at NARA document workers and families. Walker Lake attracted Paiute communities, and BIA records for the Walker River agency cover families in this area. The Mineral County Recorder in Hawthorne has deeds from 1911. Mining claims in the Candelaria, Rawhide, and Luning districts are filed at the county recorder. The Mineral County Museum in Hawthorne has local history collections. FamilySearch has limited microfilmed records given the county's late formation and small population. Check Esmeralda County for Hawthorne-area records before 1907 (when county seat was briefly there) and Lyon County for pre-1911 western portion records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: No online access; county recorder in Hawthorne handles in-person requests

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deeds from county formation. Mining claims from Candelaria, Rawhide, and Luning districts. Pre-1911 in Esmeralda or Lyon County.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete from 1911.

source_profiles.source_name: Hawthorne Army Ammunition Depot Civilian Employment Records
source_profiles.repository: NARA San Bruno / National Personnel Records Center
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Civilian personnel files from the Hawthorne Naval Ammunition Depot. Documents workers and families who lived in Hawthorne during depot operations.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — federal employment records at NARA and NPRC.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from county formation. Pre-1911 marriages in Esmeralda or Lyon County records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good from 1911.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: First enumerated as Mineral County in 1920. Pre-1911 area in Esmeralda or Lyon County census.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete for available census years.

source_profiles.source_name: Walker Lake Bulletin and Hawthorne Newspapers
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Hawthorne-area newspapers from county formation era. Military base news, mining notices, obituaries.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Limited digitization; microfilm at Nevada State Library.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major fire losses at the county level. Some mining camp records lost with camp abandonment.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed from Esmeralda County in 1864. Named for James Nye, first territorial governor. Nevada's largest county by area (18,159 sq mi — larger than some states). County seat moved from Ione to Belmont (1867), then to Tonopah (1905). Tonopah silver boom (1900–1920) revitalized the county. Includes former Nevada Test Site (now NNSS). Lincoln County (1866) and Clark County (1909) were partly carved from areas once in Nye.
county_metadata.research_tips: Nye County's massive area and multiple county seat moves mean records are scattered. Belmont was county seat from 1867–1905, and some older records remain at the Belmont courthouse (now a state park). Tonopah's silver boom (1900–1920) is well documented — the Tonopah Mining Company records at UNR Special Collections include payrolls, employment applications, and mine accident reports that name thousands of workers. The county recorder in Tonopah has mining claims from across the county. The Central Nevada Museum in Tonopah has an excellent local history collection. Nye County encompasses numerous ghost towns (Belmont, Rhyolite, Bullfrog, Manhattan, Round Mountain) — check mining district records at the county recorder for each district. The Nevada Test Site (1951+) brought federal workers to the county, documented in DOE records. Pahrump in southern Nye County is now the population center — it grew significantly after the 1970s. FamilySearch has microfilmed early Nye County records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County recorder deed and mining claim records; limited online access

source_profiles.source_name: Nye County Recorder — Land and Mining Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deeds and mining claims from county formation. Covers Nevada's largest county. Multiple mining district records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete — records preserved through county seat moves.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from county formation through mid-20th century.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — continuous from early records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census 1870–1950 (1890 destroyed). Note boom-bust population patterns — small in 1870, larger in 1910 (Tonopah).
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete for surviving years.

source_profiles.source_name: Nye County District Court Records — Belmont and Tonopah
source_profiles.repository: Nevada State Archives / Nye County Courthouse, Tonopah
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Civil and criminal case files including mining dispute litigation and probate matters. Belmont-era records partially at state archives.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — older records transferred to state archives.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Tonopah boom-era newspapers with mining news, legal notices, obituaries, and labor reports.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good digitization for Tonopah papers. Earlier Belmont-era papers have limited survival.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, estates, guardianships. Filed at Belmont through 1905, then Tonopah.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well preserved through county seat moves.

county_metadata.record_losses: No record losses. Pre-1919 records are in Humboldt County (Winnemucca).
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed from Humboldt County in 1919. Named for Gen. John J. Pershing. Lovelock is the county seat and primary population center. The Humboldt Sink and Lovelock Cave (famous archaeological site) are in the county. Agriculture (alfalfa, cattle) and mining drive the economy.
county_metadata.research_tips: Pershing County is Nevada's newest county (formed 1919), so all pre-1919 records are in Humboldt County (Winnemucca). The Pershing County Recorder in Lovelock has deeds only from 1919 onward. Lovelock was a Central Pacific Railroad stop, and railroad employment records at the California State Railroad Museum document workers in this area. Lovelock Cave, one of North America's most important archaeological sites, generated ethnographic studies of Paiute and Shoshone peoples. The Pershing County Museum in Lovelock has local family files and pioneer histories. For immigrant families, check naturalization records in both Humboldt County (pre-1919) and Pershing County courts. The Imlay and Unionville mining districts predate the county — their mining claim records are in Humboldt County's recorder office. FamilySearch has limited microfilmed records given the county's late formation.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: No online access; county recorder in Lovelock handles in-person requests. Pre-1919 records in Humboldt County.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deeds from county formation. Pre-1919 records in Humboldt County.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete from 1919.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from county formation. Pre-1919 marriages in Humboldt County records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good from 1919.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: First enumerated as Pershing County in 1920. Pre-1919 area in Humboldt County census.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete for available census years.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, estates, guardianships from county formation. Pre-1919 probate in Humboldt County.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete from 1919.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Lovelock-area newspaper with agricultural, railroad, and mining news. Legal notices and obituaries.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial digitization; microfilm at Nevada State Library.

county_metadata.record_losses: Virginia City suffered devastating fires in 1875 ('Great Fire') that destroyed much of the town, but the courthouse and its records survived. Some private and business records were lost.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of Nevada's original nine counties created in 1861. Named for Capt. Edward Storey, killed in the Pyramid Lake War. Virginia City and the Comstock Lode were the engine that drove Nevada statehood. Smallest county in Nevada by area. Population peaked at ~25,000 in 1876 and is now under 4,000.
county_metadata.research_tips: Storey County and Virginia City are the heart of Nevada's Comstock Lode silver mining history (1859–1890s), and the density of surviving records is exceptional for a western mining community. The Storey County Recorder has mining claims, deeds, and associated documents from 1861 — the volume of mining claim records is enormous. Virginia City's Great Fire of 1875 destroyed much of the town but the courthouse survived intact. The Nevada Historical Society in Reno holds extensive Comstock-era manuscript collections including mining company records, personal papers, and photographs. Virginia City had a significant Chinatown — check Chinese Exclusion Act files at NARA San Bruno and Virginia City archaeological reports. The Territorial Enterprise newspaper (where Mark Twain worked) is a premier genealogical source for the 1860s–1870s and is digitized. The Storey County Courthouse has the original records of the Comstock mining bonanza including litigation files between mining companies that name workers, engineers, and investors. The Virginia & Truckee Railroad records at the Nevada State Railroad Museum document employees.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County recorder deed and mining claim index; limited online access, extensive in-person holdings

source_profiles.source_name: Storey County Recorder — Land and Mining Claim Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Massive collection of Comstock Lode mining claims, deeds, and associated records. Among the most voluminous mining claim records in the American West.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete — courthouse survived the Great Fire of 1875.

source_profiles.repository: Chronicling America / Nevada State Library / UNR
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Nevada's most famous newspaper, where Mark Twain wrote (1862–1864). Mining stock reports, legal notices, obituaries, social news, and territorial politics.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent digitization. One of the most important sources for Comstock-era genealogy.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from territorial era. Large volume during Comstock boom (1862–1885).
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — continuous from territorial era.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census 1870–1950 (1890 destroyed). 1870 and 1880 show peak Comstock population with Irish, Cornish, Chinese, and other immigrant groups.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete for surviving years. 1870 census is especially rich for the Comstock.

source_profiles.source_name: Virginia City Chinatown — Chinese Exclusion Act Case Files
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Case files documenting Chinese residents of Virginia City's Chinatown. Includes merchant certificates, laborer certificates, and deportation files.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — NARA San Bruno holds extensive Nevada Chinese exclusion records.

source_profiles.source_name: Storey County District Court — Mining Litigation Records
source_profiles.repository: Storey County Courthouse, Virginia City / Nevada State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: District court records including major Comstock mining company lawsuits, claim disputes, and probate matters. These cases name miners, engineers, investors, and expert witnesses.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent — Storey County courthouse records are remarkably complete.

source_profiles.source_name: Storey County Tax Assessment Rolls — Comstock Era
source_profiles.repository: Nevada State Archives / Storey County Courthouse
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Property and mining tax assessments listing mine owners, property holders, and their assessed values during the Comstock boom.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — state archives holds early tax rolls.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major record losses. County records well maintained since territorial era.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of Nevada's original nine counties created in 1861. Named for the Washoe people. Reno became the county seat after it was founded as a Central Pacific Railroad town in 1868. The University of Nevada, Reno (1874) is here. Reno became internationally famous for 'quickie divorces' starting in 1906 (and especially after 1931 six-week residency law).
county_metadata.research_tips: Washoe County and Reno are the second-largest population center in Nevada, and the combination of divorce records, university archives, and the Nevada Historical Society makes it an essential research hub. Reno's divorce industry (1906–1970s) generated millions of court records that document people from across the country who established temporary residency — these are at the Washoe County District Court and, for older records, Nevada State Archives. The UNR Special Collections holds mining company records, Basque studies materials, and manuscript collections for all of Nevada. The Nevada Historical Society in Reno is the state's oldest cultural institution and holds photograph collections, family files, and manuscript groups covering the entire state. The Washoe County Recorder has deeds from the territorial era. The Truckee Meadows area attracted Chinese railroad workers, Basque sheepherders, and Italian immigrants — each community has distinct record sources. FamilySearch has microfilmed extensive Washoe County records. The Washoe County Library system has a strong Nevada genealogy collection.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Online grantor/grantee index and document images; comprehensive online access

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deeds from territorial era through present. Online index and images available.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete — well maintained since territorial era.

source_profiles.source_name: Washoe County District Court — Divorce Records
source_profiles.repository: Washoe County District Court, Reno / Nevada State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Reno was the 'Divorce Capital of America.' Millions of divorce decrees from people establishing six-week residency. These records document families from across the U.S.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent — voluminous and well preserved. Older records at Nevada State Archives.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from territorial era. Reno also attracted destination weddings.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — continuous from early settlement.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census 1870–1950 (1890 destroyed). Note diverse immigrant population: Chinese, Basque, Italian, Irish.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete for surviving census years.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, estates, guardianships from territorial era through present.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete and well preserved.

source_profiles.source_name: Reno Gazette-Journal and Nevada State Journal
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Major Reno newspapers with statewide coverage. Legal notices, obituaries, divorce listings, and community news.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good digitization coverage. UNR has additional microfilm holdings.

source_profiles.repository: Nevada State Archives / NARA San Bruno
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Naturalization petitions, declarations of intention, and certificates from Washoe County courts. Documents Basque, Chinese, Italian, and other immigrant families.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — older records at state archives.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major fire losses at the county level. Hamilton (original county seat) declined and some records were moved to Ely.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed from Lander County in 1869. Named for the White Pine Range. Hamilton (Treasure Hill silver rush, 1868) was the original county seat; seat moved to Ely in 1887. The Kennecott/Nevada Consolidated Copper Company operation at Ruth and McGill (1906–1978) was a major employer. The Nevada Northern Railway connected Ely to the main rail lines.
county_metadata.research_tips: White Pine County research divides into two eras: the Hamilton/Treasure Hill silver rush (1868–1875) and the copper mining era centered on Ely, Ruth, and McGill (1906–1978). The Hamilton courthouse records were transferred to Ely when the county seat moved in 1887. The White Pine County Recorder in Ely has deeds and mining claims from 1869. The Nevada Northern Railway Museum in Ely preserves railroad employment records and company town documentation. The Kennecott Copper operation at McGill included a company smelter town — records of Greek, Serbian, Japanese, and other immigrant workers are at UNR Special Collections and the White Pine Public Museum. The McGill Drugstore Museum and Ruth Mining District records document the copper mining community. The Ely Shoshone Tribe and Duckwater Shoshone Tribe reservations are in White Pine County — BIA records at NARA cover these communities. FamilySearch has microfilmed county vital records. Check Lander County for pre-1869 records of the White Pine area.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County recorder deed index; limited online access, most records require in-person visit

source_profiles.source_name: White Pine County Recorder — Land and Mining Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deeds and mining claims from county formation. Hamilton/Treasure Hill mining district records and Kennecott copper era claims.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete — records preserved through county seat move from Hamilton to Ely.

source_profiles.source_name: Kennecott Copper / McGill Smelter Employee Records
source_profiles.repository: UNR Special Collections / White Pine Public Museum
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Employment records from the Nevada Consolidated / Kennecott copper mining operation. Documents Greek, Serbian, Japanese, Italian, and other immigrant workers at McGill and Ruth.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial — UNR has collected significant materials. Some records at White Pine Public Museum.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from county formation. Note diverse immigrant surnames in copper mining era (1906+).
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — continuous from 1869.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census 1870–1950 (1890 destroyed). 1870 shows Hamilton boom; 1910–1940 show copper mining era with diverse immigrant workforce.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete for surviving years.

source_profiles.source_name: Ely Shoshone and Duckwater Shoshone — BIA Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Bureau of Indian Affairs records for Shoshone communities in White Pine County. Census rolls, allotment records, and agency correspondence.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — NARA holds BIA records for eastern Nevada agencies.

source_profiles.source_name: White Pine News and Ely Daily Times
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Eastern Nevada newspapers covering mining, railroad, and copper smelting news. Legal notices, obituaries, community events.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial digitization; microfilm at Nevada State Library and UNR.
